variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Ice floats because frozen water is puffed up with tiny gaps.

- When water freezes, its molecules lock into a honeycomb-like pattern with spaces.
- That makes ice take up more room, so it’s lighter for its size (less dense) than liquid water.
- Things that are less dense than the liquid float—so ice floats.
- Bonus: Ice on top keeps the water below warmer, helping fish survive in winter.", "query": "Why does ice float?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:13.475959", "duration_ms": 8369}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Birds fly by using their bodies like little airplanes.

- Wings: Their wings are curved on top. When they move forward, air pushes up on the wings and lifts them.
- Flapping: They flap to push air down and back, which makes them go up and forward.
- Feathers: Feathers spread and twist to steer and to go faster or slower.
- Tail: The tail works like a rudder to turn and to brake.
- Light bodies: Hollow bones and strong chest muscles help them fly without getting too tired.

Try this: Hold a strip of paper to your bottom lip and blow over the top. See how it lifts? That’s like a bird’s wing getting lift from moving air.", "query": "How do birds fly?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:15.206291", "duration_ms": 8271}

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Short answer: Really, really big!

- The whole universe might be endless (infinite). We don’t know yet.
- The part we can see is called the “observable universe.” It’s about 93 billion light‑years wide.
- A light‑year is how far light travels in one year—an extremely huge distance.
- Space is stretching, so the universe is getting bigger all the time.

Mind-blowing, right?", "query": "How big is the universe?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:15.445750", "duration_ms": 5837}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "- A magnet has two ends called poles: a north pole and a south pole.
- Opposite poles pull together (north + south). The same poles push apart (north + north, south + south).
- Around a magnet is an invisible “force field” called a magnetic field. It’s what pulls paperclips and fridge doors.
- Some metals stick to magnets, like iron and nickel. Plastic and wood don’t.
- Inside the magnet, tiny bits line up the same way. When they line up, the magnet gets strong.
- Earth is a giant magnet, so a compass points to Earth’s north.
- Heat or hard drops can make a magnet weaker.
- Bonus: If you wrap wire around a nail and connect it to a battery, you make an electromagnet. It works while the electricity flows.", "query": "How do magnets work?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:17.514782", "duration_ms": 8674}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "- A black hole is a place in space where gravity is so strong that nothing—not even light—can escape.
- It’s made when a very big star runs out of fuel and collapses in on itself.
- The “edge” of a black hole is called the event horizon. If something crosses it, it can’t get back out.
- We can’t see black holes directly, but we see how they pull on nearby stars and gas.
- They come in different sizes—from small ones to giant ones in the centers of galaxies.
- Don’t worry: the known black holes are very far away from Earth.", "query": "What is a black hole?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:18.314256", "duration_ms": 10370}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Stars twinkle because we’re looking at them through Earth’s shaky air.

- The air above us has moving pockets of warm and cool air. They bend the star’s light a tiny bit as it travels to your eyes.
- As the air wiggles, the light wiggles too, so the star looks like it’s flickering.
- Near the horizon (low in the sky), stars twinkle more because their light passes through more air.
- Planets usually don’t twinkle much—they look bigger to us, so the bending evens out.
- In space, without air, stars don’t twinkle at all.", "query": "Why do stars twinkle?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:18.767933", "duration_ms": 5607}

variables.result: {"websearch_response": "We don\u2019t know how big the whole universe is\u2014it might go on forever.\n\nBut the part we can see (the \u201cobservable universe\u201d) is about 93 billion light\u2011years across. A light\u2011year is how far light goes in a year\u2014about 6 trillion miles (10 trillion km). So\u2026 it\u2019s unbelievably, unbelievably big!", "query": "How big is the universe?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:18.787021", "duration_ms": 7278}

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Short answer: Pressure.

- Deep underground, rocks melt into hot liquid called magma.
- Magma has lots of gas bubbles, like a shaken soda.
- The gas and magma push upward and build pressure.
- When they find a crack or the pressure gets too big—boom!—the volcano erupts.

Extra facts:
- Where Earth’s plates pull apart, magma can rise and ooze out.
- Where plates crash together, magma can get thick and trap more gas—these eruptions can be explosive.
- Runny magma = gentler lava flows. Thick magma = bigger blasts.", "query": "What makes a volcano erupt?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:18.810936", "duration_ms": 9877}

That makes ice less dense (lighter for its size) than liquid water, so it floats.", "query": "Why does ice float?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:19.062586", "duration_ms": 6334}

request.query: How does the moon change shape?
request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "The Moon doesn’t really change shape. It looks different because sunlight lights up different parts of it as it goes around Earth.

- As the Moon orbits Earth (about every 29 days), we see different amounts of its sunlit half.
- New Moon: we can’t see the lit side.
- Crescent: a tiny sliver.
- First Quarter: half a circle.
- Gibbous: mostly lit.
- Full Moon: the whole face is lit.
- Then it shrinks back (waning) through gibbous, last quarter, crescent, to new again.

Try this: In a dark room, stand by a lamp (the Sun) and hold a ball (the Moon) at arm’s length. Turn in a circle. You’ll see the “phases” appear on the ball!", "query": "How does the moon change shape?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:19.877032", "duration_ms": 7902}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Deep underground, it’s very hot. Some rock melts into magma (melted rock). Here’s what makes a volcano erupt:

- Gas bubbles build up in the magma, like a shaken soda.
- Pressure gets stronger and pushes the magma upward through cracks.
- When the pressure is too big to hold, the volcano erupts—lava, ash, and gas come out.

Extra bits:
- Where plates of Earth’s crust move or rub, it’s easier for magma to rise.
- Runny magma = gentler lava flows. Thick, sticky magma = bigger, boomier eruptions.", "query": "What makes a volcano erupt?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:20.256357", "duration_ms": 8486}

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Sleep is like charging your body’s battery.

- It gives you energy for tomorrow.
- Your brain cleans up and saves new memories, so you learn better.
- Your body fixes itself and grows.
- It keeps you healthy and in a good mood.

Quick tips: try the same bedtime each night, keep the room dark and quiet, and turn off screens a little before bed.", "query": "Why do we need sleep?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:22.043390", "duration_ms": 7168}

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Stars twinkle because Earth’s air is wiggly.  
As star light passes through pockets of warm and cool air, it gets bent a tiny bit over and over.  
That makes the light seem to jump and change brightness, so it “twinkles.”  
Stars near the horizon twinkle more because their light passes through more air.  
Planets don’t twinkle as much because they look bigger in the sky, so their light doesn’t jump around as easily.", "query": "Why do stars twinkle?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:25.211460", "duration_ms": 5339}

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Clouds are made of tiny drops of water or tiny ice crystals floating in the air.

- Warm, wet air rises and cools.
- The water vapor sticks to tiny bits of dust or pollen and turns into drops or ice.
- Lots of those together make a cloud.
- When the drops get big and heavy, they fall as rain or snow.

It’s like the fog you see, or your breath on a cold day!", "query": "What are clouds made of?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:29.445869", "duration_ms": 4224}

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Rainbows happen when sunlight hits tiny raindrops in the sky.

- Sunlight looks white, but it’s made of many colors.
- Each raindrop acts like a little prism: the light bends, bounces inside, then comes out split into colors.
- You see a rainbow when the sun is behind you and the rain is in front of you.

Try it: On a sunny day, spray a mist with a garden hose and look with the sun behind you—you might make your own rainbow!", "query": "How are rainbows formed?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:30.488801", "duration_ms": 8543}

request.query: Why do cheetahs run so fast?
request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Cheetahs are built like tiny race cars!

- They’re light with very long legs.
- Their spine is super flexible, so each leap stretches their body like a spring.
- Big lungs and a strong heart push lots of air and blood for energy.
- Their claws grip the ground like cleats, so they don’t slip.
- A long tail works like a rudder to balance and steer.

All together, this lets them sprint really fast—about as fast as a car on a city street—but only for a short burst to catch dinner.", "query": "Why do cheetahs run so fast?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:32.309636", "duration_ms": 10758}

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Birds fly by using their wings like smart, moving gliders.

- Wing shape: Curved on top, flatter on bottom. Air moves faster over the top and lifts the wing.
- Flapping: They push air down and back, which lifts them up and moves them forward.
- Feathers: Make the wings big and smooth, and can spread or fold to control speed.
- Tail: Works like a rudder to steer and to slow down when landing.
- Body: Light bones and strong chest muscles help them flap without getting too heavy.

Try this: Hold a strip of paper to your lip and blow over the top—the paper lifts. That’s like a bird’s wing!", "query": "How do birds fly?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:33.077236", "duration_ms": 12491}

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "We need sleep for the same reason phones need charging.

- It recharges our energy so we can play and learn tomorrow.
- Our brain sorts and saves memories, like filing homework.
- Our body fixes tiny damages and grows stronger.
- Sleep helps us fight germs so we don’t get sick.
- It keeps our feelings steady so we’re less cranky.
- It helps us think clearly and not be clumsy.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Rainbows happen when sunlight shines through tiny raindrops in the air.

- Sunlight has many colors mixed together.
- Each raindrop acts like a tiny prism. The light bends going in, bounces inside, then bends again coming out.
- This spreads the colors into an arc. Red is on the outside; violet is on the inside.
- You’ll see one best when the sun is behind you and the rain is in front of you.

Try it: Spray a fine mist from a hose with the sun behind you, or shine a flashlight through a glass of water onto white paper to make a mini rainbow.", "query": "How are rainbows formed?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:35.148353", "duration_ms": 6610}

variables.result: {"websearch_response": "Because sunlight is made of many colors, and the air bounces some colors around more than others. Tiny air molecules scatter the short, blue light the most, so blue light spreads all over the sky and that\u2019s what we see. At sunrise and sunset, the light travels through more air, the blue gets scattered away, and the sky looks red/orange.", "query": "Why is the sky blue?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:36.156756", "duration_ms": 7098}

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Plants don’t eat like we do. They make their own food.

- Leaves are like tiny kitchens.
- Sunlight gives them energy.
- Roots drink water from the soil.
- Leaves take in a gas from the air called carbon dioxide.
- Using the green stuff called chlorophyll, they mix water + air with sunlight to make sugar (their food) and let out oxygen.

This cooking-with-sunlight is called photosynthesis.", "query": "How do plants eat?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:36.176857", "duration_ms": 11946}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Gravity is the invisible pull that makes things fall down.

- It keeps your feet on the ground and makes dropped apples fall.
- It holds the Moon around Earth and the planets around the Sun.
- Bigger things pull harder, and things closer together feel a stronger pull.

Try it: drop a pencil—gravity is what pulls it to the floor.", "query": "What is gravity?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:37.315908", "duration_ms": 6146}

request.query: How does the moon change shape?
request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "The Moon doesn’t really change shape. It only looks different because we see different amounts of its sunlit side.

- The Sun always lights half of the Moon.
- As the Moon goes around Earth (about 1 month), we see different parts of that lit half.
- That’s why we get phases: new moon (all dark), crescent, first quarter, gibbous, full moon (all bright), then back again.

So the “shape” is just how much of the lit side we can see from Earth.", "query": "How does the moon change shape?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:37.725383", "duration_ms": 5007}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Gravity is an invisible pull that makes things attract each other.
Earth’s gravity pulls everything toward the ground, so you don’t float away and dropped things fall.
It’s why rain comes down and why the Moon goes around Earth, and planets go around the Sun.
Bigger, heavier things have stronger gravity.

Try it: hold a ball, let go—it falls. Throw it up—it slows, stops, then comes back down. That’s gravity at work.", "query": "What is gravity?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:38.179708", "duration_ms": 9983}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Rainbows happen when sunlight shines through raindrops.

- Sunlight looks white but has many colors.
- Each raindrop acts like a tiny prism: it bends the light, the light bounces inside, then comes back out.
- Different colors bend different amounts, so they spread out into a big arc in the sky.
- To see one: have the sun behind you and rain in front of you. Red is on the outside, violet on the inside.

Try it: spray a garden hose with the sun behind you—you can make a mini rainbow!", "query": "How are rainbows formed?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:38.539739", "duration_ms": 7784}

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Gravity is an invisible pull that makes things with mass attract each other.

- Earth’s gravity pulls you down, so dropped things fall and you don’t float away.
- It keeps the Moon going around Earth and the planets going around the Sun.
- The bigger (more massive) something is, the stronger its gravity.
- If you went to the Moon, you’d weigh less because its gravity is weaker.", "query": "What is gravity?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:38.836554", "duration_ms": 5852}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Stars twinkle because we’re looking at them through Earth’s wiggly air. As their light comes down, moving air layers bend it a tiny bit in different directions, so the star looks like it’s flickering.

- It’s like seeing a coin at the bottom of a swimming pool—the water makes it shimmer.
- Planets usually don’t twinkle as much because they look bigger, so the flickers even out.
- Stars twinkle more when they’re low on the horizon, where there’s more air to look through.
- In space (no air), stars don’t twinkle at all.", "query": "Why do stars twinkle?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:39.095018", "duration_ms": 5650}

request.query: Why do leaves change color in fall?
request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result: {"websearch_response": "Because fall days get shorter and cooler, trees get ready for winter. They stop making the green stuff (chlorophyll) in their leaves. When the green fades, the other colors show\u2014yellows and oranges that were there all along. Some leaves even make reds and purples, like a little \u201csunblock,\u201d before they drop off.", "query": "Why do leaves change color in fall?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:41.127830", "duration_ms": 6673}

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Short answer: computers don’t really think. They follow instructions super fast.

- A program is like a recipe: step 1, step 2, step 3.
- Inside the computer are tiny switches (transistors) that flip on or off: 1s and 0s.
- Lots of these switches together make simple yes/no decisions.
- The CPU is the “cook” that follows the recipe; memory is the “notebook” that stores stuff.
- You give input (click/type), it processes, and you see output (screen/sound).

What about AI? It’s a very fast pattern-spotter. It guesses what fits next, but it doesn’t have feelings or understanding like a person.", "query": "How do computers think?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:42.141333", "duration_ms": 18184}

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Plants don’t eat like we do—they make their own food.

- Leaves are tiny kitchens.
- They take in sunlight, water from the soil, and a gas in the air called carbon dioxide.
- A green stuff in leaves (chlorophyll) uses the sunlight to mix these together.
- This makes sugar for the plant to grow and releases oxygen into the air.

This whole process is called photosynthesis.", "query": "How do plants eat?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:44.762029", "duration_ms": 5077}

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Gravity is an invisible pull that makes things fall down.  
It pulls you, your toys, and everything toward Earth, so you don’t float away.  
It also keeps the Moon going around Earth and the planets around the Sun.  
The bigger and closer something is, the stronger its pull.

Try it: drop a ball and a crumpled paper at the same time—they both fall because of gravity!", "query": "What is gravity?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:45.396187", "duration_ms": 8024}

request.query: Why do leaves change color in fall?
request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Leaves change color in fall because the tree is getting ready for winter.

- In spring and summer, leaves use sunlight to make food. A green stuff called chlorophyll helps, so leaves look green.
- In fall, days get shorter and cooler. The tree slows down and stops making chlorophyll.
- The green fades, and other colors show:
  - Yellow and orange were hiding there all along.
  - Red and purple can form from sugars in the leaf.
  - Brown is what’s left when the leaf dries out.

Sunny days and cool nights make colors brighter. Wind and rain can make leaves fall sooner.", "query": "Why do leaves change color in fall?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:45.881232", "duration_ms": 7764}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Here’s the simple idea:

- Deep underground, it’s so hot that rock melts into magma.
- Gas bubbles build up in the magma, like bubbles in a shaken soda.
- The bubbly magma pushes up through cracks. Pressure grows.
- When the pressure gets too strong, it “pops” out of the volcano—an eruption!

Extra: Runny (thin) magma usually makes gentle lava flows. Thick, sticky magma can explode more loudly.", "query": "What makes a volcano erupt?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:46.476714", "duration_ms": 7569}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

variables.result: {"websearch_response": "Stars twinkle because their light has to pass through Earth\u2019s air. The air is always moving and has warm and cool patches, which bend the light a little in different directions. That makes the tiny point of starlight seem to jiggle and flicker. Planets twinkle less because they look a bit bigger, and in space (above the air) stars don\u2019t twinkle at all.", "query": "Why do stars twinkle?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:47.088984", "duration_ms": 5365}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Birds fly by using their wings to push air.

- Wing shape: Their wings are curved on top and flatter on the bottom. Air moves faster over the top, making lower pressure, so the wing gets lifted up.
- Flapping: When they flap, they push air down and back. The air pushes them up and forward.
- Feathers and tail: They spread, twist, and tuck feathers to steer, slow down, and land. The tail works like a rudder and a brake.
- Bird bodies: They’re light (hollow bones) with big chest muscles to power the wings.
- Gliding: Some birds ride rising warm air called thermals so they can soar without much flapping.

Try this: Hold a strip of paper to your lower lip and blow across the top. The paper lifts—that’s like a wing making lift.", "query": "How do birds fly?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:47.862306", "duration_ms": 12741}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Electricity is a kind of energy made when tiny pieces called electrons move.

- It flows through wires like water in a hose and makes things work—lights, TVs, and phones.
- It can come from power plants (like wind, solar, dams) or from batteries.
- You sometimes feel it as “static” when your hair stands up.

Safety: Never put fingers or objects in an outlet. Ask an adult for help.", "query": "What is electricity?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:49.687120", "duration_ms": 7534}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "- Computers don’t think like people. They follow instructions.
- Inside, they flip tiny switches called transistors: off or on (0s and 1s).
- Those instructions are like a recipe (called a program). The computer follows the steps very fast.
- “AI” is when a computer looks at lots of examples to find patterns and make a guess.
- They don’t have feelings or understanding; they just follow rules and data.
- If the rules or examples are bad, the computer’s answers can be wrong.

Example: When you ask a voice assistant a question, it turns your words into numbers, matches patterns it learned, and then speaks an answer.", "query": "How do computers think?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:49.800842", "duration_ms": 10420}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Short answer: Really, really big!

- The part we can see (the “observable universe”) is about 93 billion light‑years across.
- A light‑year is how far light goes in one year—about 6 trillion miles (10 trillion km).
- The whole universe might be much bigger than that, maybe even endless. We don’t know yet.
- It’s also growing all the time, like dots on a balloon moving apart as the balloon is blown up.", "query": "How big is the universe?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:50.111535", "duration_ms": 7193}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Birds fly by using their wings to push air and make lift.

- Wing shape: The top is curved. As birds move forward, air goes faster over the top and slower under the wing, pushing the bird up.
- Flapping: When they flap, they push air down and back. The air pushes them up and forward.
- Feathers: Feathers spread out to catch air. Birds can twist them to turn or slow down.
- Muscles and bones: Strong chest muscles move the wings. Light, hollow bones make flying easier.
- Steering: The tail works like a rudder to turn, balance, and brake.
- Gliding: Big birds ride rising warm air (thermals) so they can soar without flapping.

Try this simple demo: Hold a strip of paper under your lip and blow over the top—watch it lift! Then make a paper airplane and see how small wing changes help it fly.", "query": "How do birds fly?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:51.141355", "duration_ms": 14615}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "A black hole is a place in space where gravity is so strong that nothing—not even light—can escape.  
Most form when a giant star runs out of fuel and collapses in on itself.  
It has an invisible “edge” called the event horizon; if something crosses it, it can’t come back out.  
We can’t see black holes directly, but we see how they pull on nearby stars and make hot gas glow.  
They don’t roam around sucking everything up—you’d have to get very close to be in danger.  
Huge black holes live in the centers of many galaxies, including ours.", "query": "What is a black hole?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:52.458708", "duration_ms": 8855}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "- DNA is like a tiny instruction book inside almost every cell of your body.
- It tells your body how to grow, work, and what traits you have (like eye and hair color).
- You get DNA from your parents—half from each.
- Under a microscope it looks like a twisted ladder, called a double helix.
- Its code is written with four “letters”: A, T, C, and G.", "query": "What is DNA?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:52.818331", "duration_ms": 7031}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

initial_api_response.message: Search request is being processed. Results will be sent to Glific.

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "Gravity is a pull that makes things move toward each other.

- Earth’s gravity pulls you down, so you don’t float away and things fall when you drop them.
- The bigger something is, the stronger its pull. That’s why the Sun keeps planets going around it.
- Everything has a little gravity—even you!—but big things like Earth have much more.

Try it: jump up, or drop a ball. It comes back down because of gravity.", "query": "What is gravity?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:52.853019", "duration_ms": 7669}

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "- Sunlight looks white, but it’s made of all the rainbow colors.
- The air is full of tiny bits.
- Blue light has shorter waves, so those tiny bits bounce the blue light around more.
- That scattered blue light comes from all directions, so the sky looks blue.
- At sunrise and sunset, the light goes through more air, the blue gets scattered away, and we see more reds and oranges.", "query": "Why is the sky blue?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:52.970154", "duration_ms": 11351}

request.instructions: Keep it brief and simple for a child

variables.result:
{"websearch_response": "- Wings are shaped like little air scoops. When a bird moves forward, the wings push air down, and the air pushes the bird up. That push-up is called lift.
- Flapping gives speed and extra lift. Gliding is when they hold their wings out and let the air keep them up.
- Feathers are light but strong and make the wings wide. The tail works like a rudder to steer and slow down.
- Birds have light bones and big chest muscles to flap.

Try this: move your hand through the air and tilt it—feel how the air pushes it up or down? That’s like a wing!", "query": "How do birds fly?", "processed_at": "2026-01-19T10:09:53.088866", "duration_ms": 8797}
